selection_rationale: Top 3 Critical findings with the cleanest API test surface. VULN-001 (unsafe Java deserialization on /api/deserialize) is the textbook RCE and the cleanest single-body API test. VULN-002 (SQLi in /api/login) is the textbook auth-bypass and a single JSON POST. VULN-003 (SQLi in /api/search) is the textbook mass-disclosure via a query parameter. All three are Critical per the report's risk matrix. This run is API-ONLY by explicit request, so no UI cases are emitted even where a browser surface exists; the previous split run (security_testcases_3vulns_2026-07-12_split.json) already covered the UI side for VULN-002/007/008.

testcases.title: Unsafe Java deserialization in /api/deserialize rejects base64 gadget payloads (readObject removed)
testcases.classification_rationale: API-only run by explicit request. VULN-001 is intrinsically an API vulnerability (no rendered HTML surface); even if a UI case were emitted it would just be the same HTTP call, so a UI case would be redundant. Emitting only the API case is correct.
testcases.owasp_category: A08:2021 - Software and Data Integrity Failures
testcases.description: The legacy controller called ObjectInputStream.readObject() on attacker-controlled base64-decoded bytes, enabling ysoserial gadget-chain RCE (CommonsCollections6, Spring1, etc.) because Spring Boot 3.2.5 transitively pulls in vulnerable gadget classes. Post-remediation, the endpoint is restricted to MediaType.APPLICATION_JSON_VALUE and parses the body as a Map<String,Object> via Jackson. This test verifies (a) the original gadget payload is NOT executed, (b) the endpoint does not echo a Java class name that would only come from readObject, and (c) the new JSON flow still works as the positive control.
preconditions: Application is running and reachable on http://localhost:8080
Endpoint accepts only application/json; sending text/plain should fail at content negotiation

steps.action: Submit the legacy base64-encoded ysoserial-style payload (text/plain Content-Type) to confirm the endpoint no longer accepts the original readObject surface.
steps.input_data: PAYLOAD_B64=$(printf 'aced0005' | xxd -r -p | base64); curl -sS -i -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/deserialize -H 'Content-Type: text/plain' --data-binary "$PAYLOAD_B64"
steps.expected_result: HTTP 415 Unsupported Media Type (the controller no longer consumes text/plain). No Java class name is echoed in the body, no ClassNotFoundException, no gadget execution.

steps.action: Submit the same base64 garbage but with Content-Type: application/json to confirm the JSON parser treats the body as opaque and never reaches an ObjectInputStream.
steps.input_data: PAYLOAD_B64=$(printf 'aced0005' | xxd -r -p | base64); curl -sS -i -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/deserialize -H 'Content-Type: application/json' --data-binary "$PAYLOAD_B64"
steps.expected_result: HTTP 400 Bad Request (Jackson cannot parse the base64 blob as JSON). The response body is a Jackson-style parse error. No Java class name in the response, no class-loading side effect.

steps.action: Submit a malicious-looking JSON object whose @type / class field historically would have triggered polymorphic deserialization. The Jackson default-typing global config is fail-on-unknown-properties, so undeclared fields are rejected.
steps.expected_result: HTTP 400 Bad Request (unknown property '@type' or 'cmd' rejected). No Runtime.getRuntime() invocation; no calc.exe / process spawn. The 'safe' canary is irrelevant because the request is rejected outright.

steps.action: Positive control: submit a well-formed benign JSON object and confirm the new JSON flow round-trips.
steps.expected_result: HTTP 200 with body {"type":"Map<String,Object>","size":3} (or equivalent JSON shape: a type marker and a size). No 4xx, no 5xx, no class name.

testcases.expected_result: Every malicious or malformed payload is rejected (415 or 400) and no Java class name is ever echoed. The benign JSON control returns 200 with a type-and-size acknowledgement.
testcases.pass_criteria: All three negative payloads are rejected at the HTTP layer (415/400). The positive JSON control returns 200 with a non-empty Map. The body never contains a Java class name string.
testcases.fail_criteria: Any payload returns 200 with a Java class name, OR the server executes a side effect (process spawn, file write), OR a stack trace leaks a gadget class name, OR the positive control regresses to 4xx/5xx.
testcases.remediation_reference: The readObject sink is removed. The controller now consumes application/json and uses ObjectMapper.readValue(body, Map.class). Confirm fail-on-unknown-properties is enabled globally so undeclared fields are rejected.

testcases.title: SQL injection in /api/login does not bypass authentication (parameterised query + BCrypt)
testcases.classification_rationale: API-only run by explicit request. VULN-002 has a real browser surface (/login Thymeleaf form posts to /api/login), but the previous split run already covered the UI side as SEC-002-UI. This run emits only the API case to verify the fix holds at the JSON contract layer.
testcases.description: loginUnsafe historically concatenated username and password into a native SQL string passed to EntityManager.createNativeQuery(...). The tautology ' OR '1'='1 short-circuited the WHERE clause and returned the first user row. Post-remediation, the query is parameterised (:username) and the password is compared against a BCrypt hash via PasswordEncoder.matches(). This test verifies (a) the SQLi tautology is rejected with 401, (b) a UNION-based probe leaks no SQL error, and (c) a valid seeded credential still authenticates.
preconditions: Application is running and reachable on http://localhost:8080
Seeded users exist (alice/alice123, admin/admin123) with BCrypt-hashed passwords

steps.action: Positive control: log in as alice with the correct seeded password to prove the endpoint is reachable and the BCrypt path works.
steps.expected_result: HTTP 200 with JSON body containing alice's id, username, and role. The 'password' field is absent (VULN-009 also remediated).

steps.action: Submit a SQLi tautology as the username to confirm the parameterised query treats it as a literal value.
steps.expected_result: HTTP 401 with body {"error":"Invalid credentials"} (or equivalent). No user row echoed. No 200.

steps.action: Submit a UNION-based probe to check for SQL error leakage or column-count leak.
steps.input_data: curl -sS -i -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/login -H 'Content-Type: application/json' --data-raw '{"username":"' UNION SELECT 1,2,3,4,5 FROM users--","password":"x"}'
steps.expected_result: HTTP 401, no Hibernate/SQL/ORA error string in the response body, no column-count leak in the body, no user row.

steps.action: Submit a SQLi payload in the PASSWORD field (different vector on the same fix).
steps.expected_result: HTTP 401. The hash compare is constant-time; the literal SQL string never reaches a query.

steps.action: Positive control: log in as admin to confirm the fix did not break the seeded credentials path or the password-hash check.
steps.expected_result: HTTP 200 with admin role echoed in the JSON body.

testcases.expected_result: All SQLi payloads (tautology, UNION, password-field) receive 401. The two positive controls (alice/alice123 and admin/admin123) return 200 with id/username/role. The response body never contains a user row from a SQLi payload, never contains an SQL error, and never contains the password field.
testcases.pass_criteria: Every SQLi payload returns 401. Both positive controls return 200. No SQL error string, no user row, and no password field appears in any response body.
testcases.fail_criteria: Any SQLi payload returns 200 with a populated user body, OR any response body contains a Hibernate/SQL error string, OR the password field appears in a 200 body, OR either positive control regresses to 401.
testcases.remediation_reference: Replace createNativeQuery concatenation with the parameterised query (':username' + setParameter) and verify the password against a BCrypt hash via PasswordEncoder.matches(). The VULN-009 fix removes the password field from the response body.

testcases.title: SQL injection in /api/search does not dump the users table (parameterised query)
testcases.classification_rationale: API-only run by explicit request. VULN-003 is intrinsically an API vulnerability (it is exercised purely through the ?q= query parameter on a JSON endpoint); no UI surface exists. Emitting only the API case is correct.
testcases.description: findByUsernameUnsafe historically concatenated the q parameter into a native SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '...' string. A tautology dumped every user row including the password field. Post-remediation, the query is parameterised (':username') so the q value is bound as a literal. This test verifies (a) the tautology returns zero rows (not the full table), (b) a UNION probe leaks no SQL error and returns zero rows, and (c) an exact-match query for an existing username returns that single row (positive control).
preconditions: Application is running and reachable on http://localhost:8080
Endpoint requires authentication (HTTP Basic with alice/alice123 is acceptable per the SecurityConfig)

steps.action: Positive control: search for an exact username and confirm the safe path returns exactly one row.
steps.expected_result: HTTP 200 with a JSON array of length 1 containing alice. No other users in the array. The 'password' field, if present, is a BCrypt hash (starts with $2a/$2b/$2y) - never the plaintext 'alice123'.

steps.action: Submit the original mass-disclosure tautology as the q parameter.
steps.expected_result: HTTP 200 with a JSON array of length 0. The parameterised query treats the input as a literal username (which is ' OR '1'='1) and finds no row matching that literal. The full user table is NOT dumped.

steps.action: Submit a UNION-based probe to confirm no SQL error leakage and no column-count leak.
steps.expected_result: HTTP 200 with a JSON array of length 0. No SQL/Hibernate/JPA error string in the body. No column-count leak (the array length is 0, not a multiple of the column count).

steps.action: Submit a wildcard-ish payload '%' to confirm the parameter is treated as a literal (no LIKE expansion).
steps.expected_result: HTTP 200 with a JSON array of length 0 (the literal username is '%' which matches no user). The endpoint does not perform a LIKE '%' expansion that would dump all rows.

steps.action: Positive control: search for a different exact username to confirm the safe path works for non-admins too and does not leak any password plaintext.
steps.expected_result: HTTP 200 with a JSON array of length 1 containing bob. The 'password' field, if present, is a BCrypt hash - never the plaintext 'bob123'. No other user rows in the array.

testcases.expected_result: Every SQLi payload (tautology, UNION, wildcard) returns a JSON array of length 0. The two positive controls (q=alice, q=bob) each return a JSON array of length 1. No plaintext password ever appears in any response body. No SQL error string ever appears in any response body.
testcases.pass_criteria: All three negative payloads return a JSON array of length 0. Both positive controls return a JSON array of length 1 with the correct user. No plaintext password and no SQL error string appears in any body.
testcases.fail_criteria: The tautology returns more than 0 rows (mass disclosure), OR any response body contains a plaintext password (alice123/bob123/admin123), OR any response body contains an SQL/Hibernate error string, OR either positive control regresses to length 0 or an error.
testcases.remediation_reference: Replace the createNativeQuery concatenation in UserService.findByUsernameUnsafe with the parameterised query (':username' + setParameter). VULN-009's fix keeps the password hash out of the response even though findAll/findById return full User entities.
